title: Explicit Quantum Search Algorithm for the Densest k-Subgraph Problem
authors: Yu. A. Biriukov, R. D. Morozov, I. V. Dyakonov, S. S. Straupe
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of finding the densest $k$-vertex subgraph in an arbitrary graph. This problem is NP-hard and has important applications in social network analysis, fraud detection, recommendation systems, and bioinformatics. We propose two quantum approaches to solve this problem: reduction to Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) and using Grover's quantum search algorithm. For the latter approach, we present an explicit gate-based oracle circuit utilizing Dicke states and Quantum Fourier Transform for edge counting. Numerical simulations demonstrate a quadratic speedup over classical Brute-force search.
summary: This paper presents a new quantum algorithm for the NP-hard densest k-subgraph problem using Grover's search and Dicke states. It demonstrates a quadratic speedup over classical brute-force search and provides an explicit gate-level implementation for the edge-counting oracle. The method avoids the complexities of QUBO mapping and is applicable to other graph optimization tasks.
main_problem: Finding the densest k-vertex subgraph in an undirected graph, which is an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem.
main_result: The authors propose a quantum search algorithm that provides a provable quadratic speedup over classical brute-force search, with an oracle cost scaling as O(k^2 sqrt(N)).
method: The approach uses a Grover-based search framework with Dicke state preparation, a QFT-based edge-counting oracle, and an iterative thresholding strategy.
model_or_system: The computational model is a circuit-based quantum computer utilizing a vertex register of n qubits and an edge counter register of L+1 qubits, operating on an undirected graph G=(V, E) represented by an adjacency matrix.
key_observables: Edge count within the subgraph and the oracle call cost (number of queries required for a 95% success probability).
important_parameters: Number of vertices (n), subgraph size (k), search space size (N = n choose k), and the penalty coefficient (lambda) for the QUBO formulation.
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes the availability of efficient Dicke state preparation and that the high T-count/gate depth of the QFT-based oracle can be managed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Grover iteration sequence; Figure 2 details the gate-level oracle scheme; Figures 3-5 illustrate the diffusion operator, convergence of edge counts, and numerical simulations; Figure 6-7 show the scaling of oracle costs relative to the search space size.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the DkS problem and its applications, reviews existing QUBO and variational methods, proposes a new Grover-based algorithm with explicit oracle construction, presents numerical simulations comparing the algorithm to classical baselines, and concludes with scaling analysis and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: General scheme of a single Grover-based search call at threshold mi.
FIG. 4: Benchmark graph with n = 10 vertices used in the convergence experiments.

FIG. 5: Convergence of the best-so-far edge count for the four algorithms on the benchmark graph from Fig. 4. From left to right: k = 4, 5, 6. Solid lines show the mean over 1000 independent runs, and shaded regions indicate empirical 90% confidence intervals.

FIG. 7: Comparison of oracle cost scaling for the Grover-based search and classical baselines as a function of the search-space size N = �n k  . Circles show the Grover-based oracle cost needed to reach a 95% success probability, while square markers correspond to Simulated annealing tuned to the same target success probability. The black curve represents classical Brute-force search with 95% success probability. The colored lines show power-law fits of the form y = aN b, with the fitted exponent b given in the legend.

FIG. 6: Scaling of the oracle cost of the Grover-based search as a function of the search-space size N = �n k  for several values of k. Each point shows the average number of oracle calls needed to certify the optimal densest k-subgraph with probability at least 95%; error bars indicate 99% hierarchical bootstrap confidence intervals. Here QG denotes the full Quantum Grover simulation and BBG the Black-box Grover emulator. The lines show power-law fits of the form y = aN b, with the fitted exponent b given in the legend.
